[ Research taste ]

Research taste

This is the public source trail behind future theoretical CS notes: problems, methods, and papers that shape what gets studied next.

01

NP-completeness and reductions

The clearest lens for seeing when many different-looking problems share one computational obstruction. This is the backbone for writing notes that move between intuition, formal reductions, and proof obligations.

  • Stephen Cook, The Complexity of Theorem-Proving Procedures
  • Richard Karp, Reducibility Among Combinatorial Problems
02

Shortest paths and graph structure

Shortest path algorithms expose the connection between invariants, data structures, and graph modeling. They also make implementation tradeoffs concrete without losing the mathematical thread.

  • Edsger W. Dijkstra, A note on two problems in connexion with graphs
  • Robert Tarjan, Data Structures and Network Algorithms
03

Depth-first search and graph decompositions

DFS is small enough to teach carefully and powerful enough to generate bridges, components, topological order, and linear-time graph structure. It rewards exact invariants.

  • Robert Tarjan, Depth-first search and linear graph algorithms
04

Network flow and cuts

Flow turns algorithm design into a conversation between primal constructions and dual certificates. It is one of the best places to show why correctness proofs can feel inevitable.

  • L. R. Ford and D. R. Fulkerson, Maximal Flow Through a Network
05

Dynamic programming and optimal substructure

Dynamic programming is where informal pattern recognition needs discipline: state definitions, recurrence validity, boundary cases, and reconstruction all have to line up.

  • Richard Bellman, Dynamic Programming
06

Space-bounded computation

Space complexity changes the shape of what a computation is allowed to remember. It is a useful counterweight to time-only algorithm discussions.

  • Walter Savitch, Relationships between nondeterministic and deterministic tape complexities
07

Primality and randomized thinking

Primality testing is a strong route into randomized algorithms, derandomization questions, and the surprise value of a deterministic polynomial-time proof.

  • Manindra Agrawal, Neeraj Kayal, and Nitin Saxena, PRIMES is in P
08

Matrix methods and fast algebra

Fast algebra is a reminder that basic operations hide deep algorithmic structure. It creates a bridge from algorithms into complexity and numerical computation.

  • Volker Strassen, Gaussian elimination is not optimal
09

Amortized data structures

Amortized analysis teaches readers to reason about sequences rather than isolated operations. It is one of the best techniques for turning messy behavior into a crisp bound.

  • Daniel Sleator and Robert Tarjan, Self-Adjusting Binary Search Trees
10

Algorithms as a discipline

The site needs a stable center of gravity: precise algorithm statements, correctness arguments, complexity analysis, and implementation realism in the same frame.

  • Thomas H. Cormen, Charles E. Leiserson, Ronald L. Rivest, and Clifford Stein, Introduction to Algorithms
11

Randomized algorithms

Randomness makes probability a design tool rather than a post-hoc analysis layer. It is useful for explaining expected bounds, concentration, and failure probability.

  • Rajeev Motwani and Prabhakar Raghavan, Randomized Algorithms
12

Computational complexity as a map

Complexity theory gives the notes a shared coordinate system for reductions, complete problems, randomized classes, space bounds, and what counts as evidence of hardness.

  • Sanjeev Arora and Boaz Barak, Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach

/ Subscribe

One rigorous theoretical CS deep dive every 3-4 weeks, with intuition, formal models, proof sketches, and implementation tradeoffs in one place.
